subject: ap-psychology
title: Motivation, Personality, and Cognition — Concept Application
stimulus: Malia has an assignment to write a research paper for a class. She has several weeks to work on the paper before turning in the finished product.

parts.prompt: Explain how Albert Bandura’s theory of self-efficacy could apply to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that Malia believes that she has the ability to write a good  paper and that facilitates her finishing the assignment. 

OR 

The response can indicate that a lack of belief in her ability to write a good paper makes  it less likely she would finish the assignment.

required_elements: Malia believes she can write a good paper, so she does her research and gets a good grade from her teacher.
alternatives.correct_example: Malia believes she can write a good paper, so she does her research and gets a good grade from her teacher.

required_elements: Malia is unsure whether she can write a good paper, so she procrastinates and ends
alternatives.correct_example: Malia is unsure whether she can write a good paper, so she procrastinates and ends

wrong_examples: Malia is confident about her paper.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Self-efficacy is the belief in one’s own ability to perform a specific task. The response must indicate either (1) high self-efficacy: Malia believes she can write a good paper, which facilitates completing it, or (2) low self-efficacy: a lack of belief in her ability makes it less likely she finishes. Responses that refer to confidence, self-confidence, self-esteem, or general ability rather than task-specific belief do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Malia believes she can write a good paper, so she does her research and gets a good grade from her teacher.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly shows high self-efficacy (belief in ability to complete the task) facilitating completion of the assignment.

sample_responses.response: Malia is unsure whether she can write a good paper, so she procrastinates and ends up not doing well.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — low self-efficacy (doubt in ability) hinders completion, the negative direction of the concept.

sample_responses.response: Malia is confident about her paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Refers to general confidence rather than the task-specific belief in one’s own capability that defines self-efficacy.

parts.prompt: Explain how A low score on the Big Five trait of neuroticism could apply to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate a description of low neuroticism and connect it to  completion of the paper (either helping her or hindering her).

required_elements: Malia exhibits a sense of calm so she can complete her project on time.
alternatives.correct_example: Malia exhibits a sense of calm so she can complete her project on time.

wrong_examples: Malia is very diligent, which helps her complete the project.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Low neuroticism means low emotional instability — characterized by calmness, security, emotional stability, low anxiety, and low depression. The response must describe one of these characteristics and connect it to Malia’s ability (or inability) to complete the paper. Responses that describe other Big Five traits (e.g., conscientiousness/diligence) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Malia exhibits a sense of calm so she can complete her project on time.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies calmness as a feature of low neuroticism and connects it to successful completion of the assignment.

sample_responses.response: Because Malia scores low on neuroticism, she is emotionally stable and not anxious about the paper, which helps her focus and finish it.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — emotional stability and low anxiety are accurate descriptors of low neuroticism linked to task completion.

sample_responses.response: Malia is very diligent, which helps her complete the project.
sample_responses.commentary: Diligence is a feature of conscientiousness, not neuroticism. This describes the wrong Big Five trait.

parts.prompt: Explain how External locus of control could apply to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Malia believes that outcomes are determined by forces  outside of her control (e.g., fate, luck, destiny, circumstances, other people) and that  affects her completion of the paper.

required_elements: Malia thinks that fate determines her life, so she doesn’t take proactive steps to do her paper.
alternatives.correct_example: Malia thinks that fate determines her life, so she doesn’t take proactive steps to do her paper.

required_elements: Malia thinks that random factors determine her life, so she doesn’t put in the effort to complete the assignment.
alternatives.correct_example: Malia thinks that random factors determine her life, so she doesn’t put in the effort to complete the assignment.

required_elements: Malia believes that the teacher is biased against her and so she doesn’t even try to complete the paper.
alternatives.correct_example: Malia believes that the teacher is biased against her and so she doesn’t even try to complete the paper.

wrong_examples: Malia knows that she controls her destiny.
Malia is worried that she will have bad luck.
Malia has no control over how her paper is graded, so she could do poorly.
scoring_points.official_rubric: External locus of control is the belief that outcomes are determined by forces outside one’s control — fate, luck, other people, or circumstances. The response must state that Malia believes outcomes are externally determined AND show how this affects her paper completion. Responses that describe internal locus of control (she controls her own destiny) or that simply note she could get a bad grade without attributing that to an external force do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Malia thinks that fate determines her life, so she doesn’t take proactive steps to do her paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies fate as an external force and shows how that belief reduces Malia’s effort on the assignment.

sample_responses.response: Malia believes that the teacher is biased against her and so she doesn’t even try to complete the paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — attributing the outcome to another person’s bias (external force) causes Malia to disengage from the task.

sample_responses.response: Malia has no control over how her paper is graded, so she could do poorly.
sample_responses.commentary: Simply notes a possible outcome without showing Malia attributing the outcome to an external force or how this changes her behavior.

parts.prompt: Explain how Divergent thinking could apply to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Malia generates multiple or creative solutions, and this  affects her completion of the paper.

required_elements: Malia considers many different topics for her research paper/uses an innovative approach to help her to do well on the assignment.
alternatives.correct_example: Malia considers many different topics for her research paper/uses an innovative approach to help her to do well on the assignment.

wrong_examples: Malia knows there is one best way to write a good paper.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Divergent thinking is the ability to generate multiple, creative, or novel solutions to a problem (thinking outside the box). The response must show Malia producing multiple approaches or creative solutions and connect this to her paper. Responses that describe convergent thinking (finding the one correct answer or best way) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Malia considers many different topics for her research paper and uses an innovative approach to help her do well on the assignment.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly shows Malia generating multiple possible topics — a classic application of divergent thinking to a writing task.

sample_responses.response: Using divergent thinking, Malia brainstorms multiple creative angles for her paper instead of settling on the first idea she has.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — brainstorming multiple creative angles captures divergent thinking applied to the paper scenario.

sample_responses.response: Malia knows there is one best way to write a good paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Seeking a single correct answer describes convergent thinking, the opposite of divergent thinking.

parts.prompt: Explain how Efferent neurons could apply to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that efferent (motor) neurons contribute to some element of  motor movement which affects Malia’s completion of her paper.

required_elements: Malia’s efferent neurons help her move her fingers, so she can type her paper.
alternatives.correct_example: Malia’s efferent neurons help her move her fingers, so she can type her paper.

wrong_examples: Malia’s efferent neurons help her feel where the paper is.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Efferent (motor) neurons carry signals from the brain/spinal cord to the muscles, enabling voluntary movement. The response must name a specific motor action Malia performs that is necessary for completing her paper. Responses that describe sensory input (feeling, sensing) rather than motor output do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Malia’s efferent neurons help her move her fingers, so she can type her paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies typing as a motor action controlled by efferent (motor) neurons, directly linked to paper completion.

sample_responses.response: When Malia writes her paper by hand, her efferent neurons send signals to her hand muscles to control the pen movements.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — writing by hand is another specific voluntary motor action dependent on efferent neuron output.

sample_responses.response: Malia’s efferent neurons help her feel where the paper is.
sample_responses.commentary: Feeling/sensing is a function of afferent (sensory) neurons, not efferent (motor) neurons. This reverses the direction of signal transmission.

parts.prompt: Malia’s teacher, Mr. Barry, has many papers to grade. Explain how each of the following might apply to his success in grading the papers. Explain how Incentive theory relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that a desired activity or an external reward motivates Mr.  Barry, and this helps him complete the grading.

required_elements: Mr. Barry sets a goal that if he completes his grading, he can watch his favorite television show.
alternatives.correct_example: Mr. Barry sets a goal that if he completes his grading, he can watch his favorite television show.

required_elements: After every five papers he corrects, Mr. Barry rewards himself with five minutes on his favorite social media account.
alternatives.correct_example: After every five papers he corrects, Mr. Barry rewards himself with five minutes on his favorite social media account.

wrong_examples: Mr. Barry really wants to give his students feedback, so he grades all the papers in
scoring_points.official_rubric: Incentive theory holds that behavior is motivated by external rewards or desired outcomes. The response must identify a specific external reward or desired activity that motivates Mr. Barry to grade papers. Responses describing intrinsic motivation (enjoyment of grading, desire to help students) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Barry sets a goal that if he completes his grading, he can watch his favorite television show.
sample_responses.commentary: TV watching is an external incentive that motivates Mr. Barry to finish grading — a direct application of incentive theory.

sample_responses.response: After every five papers he corrects, Mr. Barry rewards himself with five minutes on his favorite social media account.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — social media access is an external reward delivered after a set amount of work, motivating continued grading.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Barry really wants to give his students feedback, so he grades all the papers in one night.
sample_responses.commentary: Wanting to help students is an intrinsic motivation. Incentive theory requires an external reward or desired activity as the motivator.

parts.prompt: Malia’s teacher, Mr. Barry, has many papers to grade. Explain how each of the following might apply to his success in grading the papers. Explain how Crystallized intelligence relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that accumulated knowledge (facts, general information, or  vocabulary) helps Mr. Barry successfully grade the papers or that a lack of accumulated  knowledge hinders his success.

required_elements: Mr. Barry can grade papers quickly because he is able to remember lots of facts about the topic he is grading.
alternatives.correct_example: Mr. Barry can grade papers quickly because he is able to remember lots of facts about the topic he is grading.

required_elements: Mr. Barry is wise because he has learned so much in his life, which helps him grade papers faster.
alternatives.correct_example: Mr. Barry is wise because he has learned so much in his life, which helps him grade papers faster.

wrong_examples: Mr. Barry grades papers faster because he is an experienced grader.
Mr. Barry is capable of thinking very quickly and thus can grade a large number of
scoring_points.official_rubric: Crystallized intelligence is accumulated knowledge — facts, vocabulary, and general information built up through experience and education. The response must show accumulated knowledge (wisdom, facts about the subject) helping (or hindering) Mr. Barry’s grading. Responses describing general intelligence or fluid intelligence (fast thinking, problem-solving speed) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Barry can grade papers quickly because he is able to remember lots of facts about the topic he is grading.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately shows accumulated factual knowledge (crystallized intelligence) enabling efficient grading.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Barry is wise because he has learned so much in his life, which helps him grade papers faster.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — wisdom from lifelong learning is a form of crystallized intelligence, applied here to grading efficiency.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Barry is capable of thinking very quickly and thus can grade a large number of papers in one night.
sample_responses.commentary: Thinking quickly describes processing speed or fluid intelligence, not crystallized intelligence (accumulated knowledge).
